does: This paper proposes a self-mining approach to safety fine-tuning that scores candidate prompts by how frequently a language model's own rollouts are judged harmful, then fine-tunes on the hardest prompts paired with the model's own safe responses rather than relying on curated adversarial datasets. The method is evaluated on Llama-3-8B-Instruct and Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct, achieving substantial reductions in WildJailbreak attack success rates from 11.5% and 20.1% down to 1–3%. However, this comes at the cost of excessive refusal on benign prompts that superficially resemble jailbreaks (rising to 74–94%), which the authors mitigate by interleaving hard adversarial prompts 1:1 with adversarially-framed benign prompts, reducing false refusals to 30–51% on 8B and 52–72% on 3B while maintaining attack success rates at 3–9%.
connects: This work extends the broader literature on adversarial robustness and safety alignment in language models, which has traditionally relied on hand-curated adversarial datasets and red-teaming. The approach builds on techniques for difficulty-based curriculum learning and hard-example mining, which have shown promise in other domains but are less established in the safety fine-tuning context. It responds directly to practical limitations of existing methods: the cost and brittleness of curated datasets, and the known tension between robustness to jailbreaks and maintaining utility on benign queries. The paper engages with problems identified in recent work on jailbreak evaluation and the distinction between harmful and benign adversarial prompts, incorporating insights about the limitations of simple refusal-based defenses.
enables: This work opens new directions for scalable safety alignment by automating adversarial example selection through self-mining rather than manual curation, potentially reducing the engineering and annotation overhead in safety fine-tuning pipelines. The interleaving strategy for balancing robustness against jailbreaks with utility on benign queries suggests a systematic framework for understanding and controlling false-refusal tradeoffs. Future work could explore whether self-mined hardness transfers across models or generalizes to new attack types, whether the hardness signal can be combined with other difficulty metrics, and how this approach scales to larger models or more diverse threat models. The method also raises questions about whether models can identify their own vulnerabilities more effectively than external red-teamers and whether curriculum-based variants could further improve the sample efficiency of safety training.
